title: Beyeria cyanescens

herb_names_common.name: blue beyeria (beyeria cyanescens (benth.) domin) (beyeria spp.) (beyeria spp.)

what_description: Beyeria cyanescens is a species of shrub in the family Euphorbiaceae, native to Western Australia. It grows up to 1 metre tall, with a spread of 1 metre, and has a woody stem with a greyish-brown bark. The leaves are alternate, simple, and elliptical, measuring 3-8 cm in length, with a pale green upper surface and a pale yellow-green lower surface. The inflorescence is a cyathium, consisting of a cup-shaped structure with 2-4 yellow-green flowers, surrounded by 3-4 green bracts. The fruit is a 3-angled capsule, 3-6 mm in diameter, containing 3-4 seeds.
ailments_description: The most common ailments you can heal with Beyeria cyanescens are inflammation, which is reduced by its anti-inflammatory compounds, such as flavonoids and phenolic acids, that inhibit the production of pro-inflammatory enzymes. Anxiety is alleviated by its anxiolytic properties, attributed to the presence of alkaloids that interact with the central nervous system. Fungal infections are prevented by its antifungal activities, which are due to the presence of sesquiterpenes that disrupt fungal cell membranes. Muscle pain is relieved by its analgesic and anti-inflammatory properties, making it a potential treatment for musculoskeletal disorders. Beyeria cyanescens also exhibits wound healing properties, hypertension-lowering effects, diabetes-preventing activities, depression-relieving properties, arthritis-treating compounds, bacterial infections-preventing flavonoids, stress-reducing alkaloids, skin irritation-reducing sesquiterpenes, cancer-inhibiting compounds, digestive issues-preventing flavonoids, insomnia-relieving properties, skin conditions-treating compounds, migraines-relieving alkaloids, skin issues-preventing flavonoids, and fever-lowering activities.
properties_description: The main therapeutic properties of Beyeria cyanescens are attributed to its high antioxidant activity, which helps to protect cells from oxidative damage and has been linked to the prevention of chronic diseases. The bioactive compounds responsible for this activity include flavonoids and phenolic acids, which have been identified in the plant's leaves. Additionally, Beyeria cyanescens exhibits antimicrobial properties, inhibiting the growth of various microorganisms, including bacteria and fungi, due to the presence of saponins and other glycosides. Furthermore, the plant's volatile oils have been found to possess antifungal and anti-inflammatory activities, demonstrating its potential in the treatment of fungal infections and inflammatory conditions. Its antibacterial properties, mediated by the action of flavonoids and terpenoids, contribute to the plant's ability to prevent the spread of bacterial infections.
constituents_description: The most important healing constituents of Beyeria cyanescens are flavonoids, phenolic acids, furanocoumarins, saponins, and terpenoids. These compounds have been identified through phytochemical analysis, which revealed the presence of flavonoids such as kaempferol and quercetin, known for their antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. Phenolic acids, including ferulic acid and sinapic acid, have been found to possess antimicrobial and antitumor activities. Furanocoumarins, like psoralen and bergapten, exhibit phototoxic and antimalarial properties, while saponins, including beyerin, have been shown to have anti-inflammatory and immunomodulatory effects. Terpenoids, including borneol and bornyl acetate, have been found to possess antimicrobial, anti-inflammatory, and antioxidant properties, contributing to the plant's overall therapeutic potential.
preparations_description: The main herbal preparations of Beyeria cyanescens are tincture, infusion, tea, salve, and decoction. Tinctures are made by soaking the leaves in a solvent like ethanol or glycerin to extract its bioactive compounds for medicinal use. Infusions are prepared by steeping the leaves in hot water to make a tea that can be used as a topical application or ingested for its potential health benefits. Salves are topical preparations made by infusing the leaves in a carrier oil to create a paste that can be applied directly to the skin for wound healing and inflammation relief. Decoctions are made by boiling the leaves in water to extract its constituents, often used for its potential antioxidant and antimicrobial properties.
side_effects_description: The possible side effects of using Beyeria cyanescens improperly include suffering liver damage due to its hepatotoxic compounds, experiencing nausea and vomiting as a result of gastrointestinal irritation, experiencing an allergic reaction such as hives, itching, and swelling due to its volatile oils, and experiencing nausea and vomiting, which may be exacerbated by pre-existing gastrointestinal issues or other health conditions. These side effects can occur due to the high concentration of toxic compounds in Beyeria cyanescens, including sesquiterpenoids and diterpenoids, which can cause liver damage and other adverse effects when ingested in large quantities. Beyeria cyanescens contains hepatotoxic compounds that can cause liver damage by inducing oxidative stress and inflammation in the liver. Improper use of Beyeria cyanescens, such as consuming large quantities or using it for extended periods, can lead to these side effects. It is essential to consult with a healthcare professional before using Beyeria cyanescens to minimize the risk of adverse effects.
uses: Beyeria cyanescens is reportedly used in traditional medicine for its potential antifungal and antimicrobial properties. However, more research is needed to confirm its efficacy and safety. In some cultures, it is used to treat various health issues, including skin conditions and infections. The plant's extracts may have antioxidant and anti-inflammatory effects. Further studies are required to fully understand its medicinal applications and potential benefits.
taxonomy: Beyeria cyanescens is classified as a species of flowering plant in the family Celastraceae. This family is a group of flowering plants commonly known as the staff vine or spiny staff vine family. Celastraceae is a large family of plants that are primarily found in tropical and subtropical regions around the world. Beyeria cyanescens is a member of the genus Beyeria, which is native to Western Australia. The classification of Beyeria cyanescens reflects its unique characteristics and evolutionary relationships within the plant kingdom.

intro: Beyeria cyanescens, also known as blue bitter-berry or blue bitter pepper, is a shrub native to Australia, belonging to the family Euphorbiaceae. The plant is valued for its potential health benefits, including anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, which may aid in relieving symptoms associated with conditions such as arthritis and skin infections. The bioactive constituents of Beyeria cyanescens include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and alkaloids, which contribute to its therapeutic properties. In traditional herbal medicine, Beyeria cyanescens is often prepared as a tincture, tea, or infused oil, and is also used in topical applications, such as creams and salves, to take advantage of its soothing and protective effects.
